title: Method and system for generation and distribution of high voltage direct current
abstract: A system for generation and distribution of high voltage direct current (HVDC) within a contained power domain named 'POD' and methods for making and using the same. The system and methods efficiently power Information Technology racks deployed to a data center environment, advantageously providing features and functions highly desirable for a specific application.
claims: Claims CLAIMS What is claimed is: 1. A high-voltage direct current (HVDC) power domain for one or more data center racks, comprising: a system power domain for receiving input from an alternating current (AC) power grid; and an HVDC busway for receiving a direct current from the system power domain for powering the one or more data center racks, wherein the system power domain consolidates server power outside of the one or more data center racks. 2. The HVDC power domain of claim 1, wherein the system power domain optionally receives 380-480 VAC input from the AC power grid and comprises: an HVDC shelf for generating the direct current from the AC power grid; and one or more batteries in operable connection to the HVDC shelf. 3. The HVDC power domain of any one of claim 1 or 2, wherein the one or more batteries are coupled to the HVDC busway via one or more intermediate converters and one or more battery dischargers and optionally are Lithium Ion battery backup units. 4. The HVDC power domain of any one of claims 1-3, wherein the HVDC shelf comprises one or more redundant high-power rectifiers and optionally produces at least 380 VDC from the AC power grid across the HVDC busway. 5. The HVDC power domain of any one of claims 1-4, wherein the one or more data center racks comprise one or more information technology racks. 6. The HVDC power domain of any one of claims 1-5, wherein the system power domain operates in at least one of a peak sharing mode, a surge mode, and a peak shaving mode. 7. The HVDC power domain of any one of claims 1-6, wherein the one or more batteries comprise three arrays in parallel, each array comprising ninety-six Li-Ion cells in series. 8. A high-voltage direct current (HVDC) cabinet for one or more data center racks, comprising: at least two system power domains for receiving input from an alternating current (AC) power grid; and an HVDC busway for receiving a direct current from at least one of the system power domains for powering the one or more data center racks, wherein each system power domain consolidates server power outside of the one or more data center racks and comprises: an HVDC shelf for generating the direct current from the AC power grid; and one or more batteries in operable connection to the HVDC shelf. 9. The HVDC cabinet of claim 8, wherein the one or more batteries are coupled to the HVDC busway via one or more intermediate converters and one or more battery dischargers, wherein an output for each of the one or more batteries optionally is connected in series to the one or more battery dischargers or connected in parallel, the batteries in parallel is coupled in series to a single input of the one or more battery dischargers. 10. The HVDC cabinet of any one of claim 8 or 9, wherein the cabinet is an open rack. 11. The HVDC cabinet of any one of claims 8-10, wherein each system power domain powers a unique set of the one or more data center racks. 12. The HVDC cabinet of any one of claims 8-11, further comprising an AC breaker hard-wired to the AC power grid and for regulating the input to each of the system power domains. 13. The HVDC cabinet of any one of claims 8-12, further comprising a solid-state switch for interconnecting the system power domains, wherein the solid-state switch optionally is a bi-directional solid switch having a 45KW rating. 14. The HVDC cabinet of any one of claims 8-13, further comprising a soft switch for each of the system power domains for turning off the batteries associated with a selected system power domain. 15. The HVDC cabinet of any one of claims 8-14, wherein each HVDC shelf comprises one or more redundant high-power rectifiers.
